entry.title: SAP Build Process Automation: Stable selection of dynamic dropdown items in SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am building an automation to handle the "Anonymise Candidate Profiles" process in SAP SuccessFactors Admin Centre. I need to search for a candidate and select the correct one from a dropdown by matching the Email ID from an Excel sheet.

Current Problem: My current logic using "Keystroke Down" is unstable; it frequently skips items or fails to update the selection. Even when using a Collection (LI) with a For Each loop, the UI does not consistently register the click, and the automation sometimes retrieves technical metadata (JSON) instead of the visible text.

Technical Details:

  • Target Element: Li captured as a collection (Class: "fd list item").

  • Logic: Iterate through the collection -> Get text -> Compare with Excel Email -> Click matching index.

  • Challenge: Standard click/keystroke activities are not triggering the SuccessFactors selection event reliably.

Question: How can I achieve a stable selection in this specific UI? Is there a recommended Custom Script or a specific UI5/web event I should trigger to ensure the dropdown value is selected and the screen refreshes for the next candidate?

Attached are my recognition criteria and flow screenshots.

Any help is appreciated!

Thanks,

Narenthiran Annamalai.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Note 3690029 – Integration Technologies and Frameworks in the Context of Clean Core
entry.content:
SAP Note 3690029 – Integration Technologies and Frameworks in Context of Clean Core Integration is now available. This blog post gives a quick overview of the note, the reasoning behind it, and how it fits next to its well-known sibling, SAP Note 3578329 for extensibility.

Why a dedicated integration note?

When customers and partners plan their journey to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition (and ultimately to SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition), one question keeps coming back:

"I want to use interface technology X – is that still considered Clean Core?"

Until now, the only widely referenced answer was SAP Note 3578329 – Frameworks, Technologies and Development Patterns in Context of Clean Core Extensibility. That note is excellent – but it was written for extensibility use cases. In day-to-day projects it has been increasingly applied to integration scenarios as well, which led to misinterpretations, and a fair amount of uncertainty in the field about what is and isn't a clean integration.

A dedicated, official statement for integration technologies was needed and that is what SAP Note 3690029 delivers.

What does the new note provide?

SAP Note 3690029 introduces a level-based Clean Core integration model and classifies the most commonly used interface technologies, protocols, and patterns according to two criteria:

  1. Public Cloud readiness: is the technology supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition?
  2. Modern integration standards: is it based on established cloud and internet standards, and does it align with modern approaches like event-driven architecture (EDA) and an API-managed strategy?

Each technology is rated on the same A / B / C / D scale already known from the extensibility note:

  • Level A – Strategic, cloud-ready, modern. Always the first choice
  • Level B – Works today, but plan to move on. Limited cloud readiness or limited alignment with modern standards
  • Level C – Legacy, no cloud migration path. Only acceptable in clearly bounded on-premise scenarios
  • Level D – Avoid in any new design

The same simple rules apply as for extensibility:

  • Stay clean: choose the highest level available for the use case.
  • Get clean: from the current level, plan the move to the next higher one.

How is this different from SAP Note 3578329?

The two notes complement each other and do not overlap. If a framework or technology touches both worlds (a classic example: IDoc integration combined with an IDoc extension), the rule of thumb is simple:

  • SAP Note 3690029 is used to choose the right integration technology.
  • SAP Note 3578329 is used to implement the extension in a clean way.
Clean Core principleIntegrationExtensibility
Typical question"How is S/4HANA accessed from another system?""How is S/4HANA itself extended?"
Example use caseCalling a released OData / web service / IDoc / BAPI from an external systemImplementing a BAdI, creating a custom CDS view

The decision tree below(also attached to the note captures the flow at a glance:

Decision tree – when to apply SAP Note 3690029 vs. SAP Note 3578329

  1. Is a released API/event on SAP Business Accelerator Hub or a standard SAP integration scenario available and sufficient? → Use it, Cleanest possible option.
  2. If not, hand over to extensibility for any local change. If standard extension points exist, they should be used and the Cloudification Repository should be followed.
  3. If no SAP standard content exists at all, SAP Note 3578329 should be followed, and only as a last resort should a custom interface be built, still respecting Clean Core extensibility guidelines.

How to use the note in projects

A few practical recommendations:

  • Apply it during integration assessment, Whenever a new interface is scoped, the technology should be looked up in the table and the resulting Clean Core level documented as part of the architecture decision.
  • Reassess regularly, Released APIs and events are continuously added to the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. What is Level B today might have a Level A successor tomorrow.
  • Combine the note with the right tooling:
    • SAP Business Accelerator Hub – the catalog of released remote APIs and events, including documentation and versioning.
    • SAP Help Portal – integration and modernization guides plus release information for SAP S/4HANA remote APIs and business events.
    • Cloudification Repository – object-level release state and Clean Core classification
  • Do not forget upgrade stability. Because upgrade stability depends on the release status of the underlying ABAP object, it is intentionally not part of the integration level rating. Cross-checking with SAP Business Accelerator Hub, the Cloudification Repository, and SAP Note 3578329 is recommended.

What's next?

The note is a living document. The current version focuses on integration technologies available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition. Future updates will:

  • Expand the table to additional interface technologies, including data integration

entry.title: Re: SAp Build Work Zone Advanced: How to call a workpage...
entry.content:
Hi Ralf,                                                                                            

                                                                                                 

  I understand your scenario - you want to use a Work Zone page as a generic detail template that's  accessible via links from Component Cards, but not visible in the navigation menu. However, hiding the page results in "Access denied" errors.                                                         

 Here are some recommended Solutions:                                                                              

                  

  Option 1: Use Page-Level Permissions (Recommended)                                                  

  - Keep the page visible in navigation

  - Apply role-based permissions to restrict who can see it in the menu                               

  - Assign access only to relevant user groups                         

  - Links will work, but only authorized users see it in navigation                                   

                                                                                                      

  Option 2: Create a Dedicated Space                                                                  

  - Move the detail page to a separate Space                                                          

  - Configure Space permissions appropriately                                                         

  - Link directly to this page from your cards                                                        

  - This Space won't appear in main navigation                                                        

                                                                                                      

  Option 3: Use URL Parameters                                                                        

  - Instead of separate pages, use a single dynamic page                                              

  - Pass parameters via URL to show summary or detail view                                            

  - Configure the card links with query parameters

entry.title: Your Journey to SAP Data Architect Certification Starts Now
entry.content:
The era of the "all-knowing" architect has arrived, and the blueprint for the enterprise of tomorrow is being written in real-time through the transformative power of Data and AI.

As a professional consultant or architect, you’ve spent your career designing complex systems, but the modern landscape now demands a visionary who can seamlessly bridge the gap between robust infrastructure and the autonomous world of Agentic AI. This is your moment to evolve from a traditional strategist into a high-impact leader capable of orchestrating a data-first world.

 

By enrolling in our newly released learning journeys: Becoming an SAP Data Architect and Mastering SAP Data Architecture. You are securing an undeniable career advantage in an AI-driven economy. These courses offer a structured, rigorous path designed specifically for experts ready to master modern paradigms like Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Zero-ETL integration. You will gain the technical skills required to design scalable, high-performance data ecosystems that facilitate real-time access and advanced analytics across hybrid cloud environments.

 

Completing this journey validates your expertise on a global stage through industry-recognized certification: the SAP Certified Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud. You will master the integration of SAP’s open data ecosystem with external giants like Databricks and Google BigQuery, ensuring you can lead complex AI initiatives with absolute confidence. The future belongs to those who have the skills to build it - claim your seat at the forefront of innovation today.

 

Start your journey today!

entry.title: Re: production order long text
entry.content:
Dear @jesseho271,

The standard Production Order long text is not supported as a writable field through the released Production Order API.
The relevant released API is Production Order Version 2 / API_PRODUCTION_ORDER_2_SRV. SAP positions this API for creating, reading, updating production orders and setting order statuses but the update capability is limited to the properties supported by the API model. SAP’s API reference marks OrderLongText as read-only, so it should not be used in a POST or PATCH payload to maintain the standard production order long text

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d35113ee62644d3abee1aaec148291d9/5d0c066e4bfa4831b3bd244e3c34e233.html

For integration scenarios the recommended Public Cloud approach is to use a custom field on the production order header and enable it for API usage. SAP confirms that the extensibility options for A_ProductionOrder_2 were enhanced so that custom fields can be included when production orders are created or updated via API_PRODUCTION_ORDER_2_SRV

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/doc/ce01d82756b947a1a043a5d5a3204226/2308.503/en-US/WN_CE2308.3_EN.pdf

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3614263

It is also important to distinguish this from Manufacturing Order Send (Version 2) used for MES integration. That service can contain LongText, but it is an outbound/send scenario to MES and does not provide an inbound API to update the standard production order long text in S/4HANA Cloud. SAP KBA 3265926 confirms this MES/outbound context and describes the LongText field there as plain text without formatting

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3265926

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP AI: Beyond the Hype and Into the Real World
entry.content:
Three days in Excel hell. Twenty supplier bids. One AI agent that knows your data. This is the SAP AI story your vendor deck won't tell you — first in a series.

I've been on the digital transformation journey for more years than I care to admit. I truly believe that constant evolution is the only way to stay relevant in procurement, but lately, it feels like the word "transformation" has been completely hijacked by a single two-letter acronym: AI. Everywhere I turn, AI is the only topic in the room. It is being pitched as the ultimate cure-all, yet for most of us, the daily reality still involves wrestling with legacy systems and messy data.

After endless conversations about AI's impact today, where it is going, and how it fits into our daily lives, I've realized that what we need is not more noise, but a clear roadmap.

My intention with this series is to provide a solid understanding of where we are today, set some realistic expectations, and point out where the magic really happens when we get it right.

We will look at what SAP is doing broadly with Business AI, while zooming in specifically on the procurement space where the impact is most immediate. Each post in this series will focus on a specific piece of the puzzle or a real-world scenario, helping you not only navigate this AI transformation but ensuring you actually see tangible value at the end of it. And I promise: no marketing fluff and no buzzword bingo.

 

The Anatomy of Success: How the Pieces Actually Fit Together

Stop thinking about AI as a single feature you can switch on. It's a high-performance system built from three distinct, non-negotiable layers. Pull any one out and the whole thing fails to deliver value. Here's the blueprint:

 The three layers of the SAP AI stack. Remove any one and the system fails to deliver value.

Each layer has a clear job. BTP is where your code actually runs and your security lives. SAP Business Data Cloud is the part that prevents your AI from getting confused — it makes sure a "Supplier" in one system is the same thing as a "Vendor" in another. Joule is the conversational front door that takes your natural-language request and orchestrates the work across every back-end system. Everything else in this post depends on these three working together.

 

Four Flavors of SAP AI You'll Actually Encounter

Not all AI is created equal — and you'll meet at least four different flavors in any real SAP deployment. Each one solves a different problem and shows up in your workflow in a different way. Here's how to recognize them:

 Four flavors of SAP AI. Each one solves a different problem — and you'll meet all of them

 

Talk Is Cheap. Let's Look at a Real Scenario.

We've all heard the lofty promises of AI, but talk is notoriously cheap. Instead of another high-level theory, I want to show you a practical scenario that has already been built and is currently saving procurement experts hundreds of hours. This is not some futuristic concept waiting for a release date — it's how we're solving real-world complexity right now.

 The Bid Analysis Agent in action — what 80% of the heavy lifting looks like.

WHAT HAPPENS TODAY WITHOUT AI

You're stuck in Excel hell for three days. You're manually pivoting data, trying to run "what if" scenarios, and likely making a few coffee-fueled typos along the way. By the time you finally have an answer, the market data has already shifted and your analysis is effectively a historical document.

THE BASIC AI EXPERIENCE

You try to use a generic, public AI tool. You upload a spreadsheet and ask for an analysis. It gives you a shallow "cheapest is best" answer, hallucinates a few shipping routes that don't exist, and potentially leaks your sensitive pricing data to the public web. Fast, but dangerous — and completely lacking business context.

THE REALISTIC SAP AI FUTURE

You use a Bid Analysis Agent grounded in your actual Ariba data. You ask Joule to show the award scenario that minimizes risk if fuel prices spike. The agent evaluates the trade-offs, highlights bid outliers, and suggests a data-driven strategy in seconds by factoring in unit prices, shipping, and risk data. You're still the decision-maker — but the AI has handled 80% of the analytical heavy lifting for you.


Straight Talk: Paying the Data Tax

AI in 2026 is not about replacing the procurement professional. It is about capacity. It's about getting that 20% to 30% of your week back from repetitive, manual work.

But let's be real about the Data Tax. I'm not suggesting every organization is starting from scratch. In fact, most customers I talk to have done a great job with their supplier and master data. The real mess usually hides elsewhere: in the offline spreadsheets, the unstructured sourcing history, and the fragmented procurement processes that haven't quite made it into the system yet.

 

Two data realities. One of them turns your AI into a confident liar.

Good data is structured, centralized, and consistent. It means having one version of the truth, so that when you ask an AI for a risk assessment, it isn't hallucinating because it found three different versions of the same contract.

Bad data is the fragmented manual sprawl — the tribal knowledge and the manual workarounds that live outside your digital core.

When you try to use AI with bad data, the implications are simple: the AI becomes "confidently wrong." It will give you an answer, but that answer will be based on a fragment of reality. Paying the Data Tax is not a one-time activity or a weekend cleanup. It's an ongoing evolution in how you store and leverage your information — a commitment to continuous improvement where your AI outputs steadily improve as your data strategy matures.

You simply cannot ignore the data layer of the architecture and expect the interaction layer to give you accurate results.

What's Next

Moving forward, this series will dive deep into the specific AI scenarios and features that drive actual savings in both time and money. I'll be sharing not just what we're building at SAP, but also the real-world best practices I'm seeing from other leading organizations that are successfully closing the "execution gap." There's a wealth of peer knowledge out there that can benefit us all.

I have specific use cases planned, but I'm always interested in your perspective. If there's a particular challenge you're facing or a piece of the puzzle you want to see solved, let me know what you'd like me to cover in future chapters.

entry.title: VIM - Issue resetting Capture Profile (IES)
entry.content:
Hello colleagues,

After the implementation of the Universal Handler (VIM version 23.4) through the creation of a new archive document type, a reset of the Profile is required in order to identify the new doc type. In VIM, during Capture Profile reset via Capture Administration, it may happen that the process gets stuck in “Resetting” status and does not move forward.

Opentext recommendation to solve the issue is:

Step A: Maintain bgRFC Configuration: /OTX/PF00_BGRFC_INB_DEST

  1. Run the SBGRFCCONF transaction.
  2. Make sure a supervisor destination is assigned. You have to create a supervisor destination if it is not available.

 3. Define an inbound destination “/OTX/PF00_BGRFC_INB_DEST” and save the changes. Optionally, you can assign a dedicated server group. Queue prefixes are not required

4. Configure Scheduler for the inbound destination, adjust settings according to the requirements, and save the changes.

 

Step B: Reset the IES profile in question like this:

  1. For safety reasons, extract and save the table /OTX/PF11_T_PROF at first: 

2.In the table manually set the status from RESETTING to ERROR.

 3. Please delete the value of the field CHANGE_ACTION.

 

 

4. After this you can reset the profile. Once the reset is completed, activate the profile again.

After performing the above steps, Profile identified the new archive document type correctly and we were able to upload invoices using the new Universal Handler.

I hope the above solution helps someone out there facing issues with IES profile reset process.

Regards to all,

Rafael

entry.title: Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions
entry.content:
Starting with AI in SAP Customer Experience Solutions

During this session, you'll gain a foundational understanding of AI concepts and how they can be applied to SAP CX solutions. We'll explore the key AI strategy and technologies available for CX, including Predictive AI, Generative AI, and Conversational AI, and how they can be leveraged to improve customer interactions, personalize experiences, and automate customer-related processes.

 

Learning Outcome

After participating in this live session, you will be able to:

  • Understand and identify key AI technologies applicable to SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions
  • Recognize specific use cases
  •  Outline a basic plan for implementing AI to enhance customer experiences in your own organization

 

Why Attend a Live Session?

This session is introductory on leveraging Artificial Intelligence (AI) within SAP Customer Experience (CX) solutions! This live session is designed for business professionals, IT specialists, and SAP users looking to understand how AI can enhance customer experiences, streamline operations, and drive growth.

 

Register Today

Register today to start building the skills that will move you forward.

entry.title: Agentic AI Meets Academia: New No-Cost Offerings for Teaching
entry.content:
AI agents are making their way into enterprise operations at remarkable speed. They reason, plan, and act within and across business processes with growing autonomy. For educators, this raises a question: how can students learn to build and govern these agents using the tools they will encounter in the industry after graduating?

This is what motivated SAP to put together a new set of academic offerings and resources that help universities embed agentic AI related concepts and technology into their teaching hands-on.

 

What Is Available Now

Three offerings, each covering a different angle of agentic AI, are accessible at no cost for academic lecturers and their students for teaching and learning:

  1. Starter resources for Agent Building: Lecturers and their students can access an agent-building environment from SAP and leverage various enablement resources. These will allow students to explore configuring and building an AI agent, either in a guided demo experience, or in a live SAP Business Technology Platform system hands-on.
  2. SAP LeanIX for lecturers: For students to understand where agents sit within an organization's IT landscape, this is the tool. Newly available at no cost for academic lecturers via SAP Learning Hub, student edition, SAP LeanIX lets students model enterprise architectures and reason about what changes when you introduce AI agents into an existing system landscape.
  3. SAP Signavio for lecturers and students: Before building an agent, the process it will operate in must be understood. The SAP Signavio Process Transformation Suite gives lecturers and their students access to process mining, modeling, and process transformation capabilities. They can model and analyze existing processes, spot inefficiencies, and design improved workflows that include AI agents. Additionally, students and lecturers can now experience process modelling with SAP Signavio Process Modeller as part of SAP Learning Hub, student edition.

What makes the new set of offerings unique, is that various related components of agentic AI can be explored hands-on using solutions from SAP. Students learn that building an agent is only part of the job. Understanding process context, architectural and governance implications is what is equally important.

Collaboration with Early Adopter Universities

SAP will also collaborate intensively on embedding agentic AI into teaching with lecturers from more than ten universities globally, such as Technical University of Munich, TEC de Monterrey, University of California, Irvine,  National University of Singapore Business Analytics Centre, HEC Montréal, Tongji University, University of Potsdam, KIT Karlsruhe, TU Dresden, Budapest University of Technology and Economics, Eötvös Loránd University.

 

"What excited me is that students get to work with enterprise-grade tools, thanks to this new platform" says Prof. Jesús Aguilar-Gonzalez, TEC de Monterrey. "Students from our School of Engineering & Sciences build agents connected to real business processes and have to think about architecture and governance. That is much closer to what they will face in their first job than any textbook exercise.”

The institutions will get exclusive early access to SAP’s latest agent building platform capabilities, benefit from agent building deep dives for students with SAP experts, and from the opportunity to articulate academic needs with regards to teaching agentic AI related concepts hands-on to SAP.

 

Why this matters to SAP

As part of the SAP University Alliances Program, SAP has been partnering with universities for decades to enable students to learn, research, and innovate with business applications and technology. The AI era requires an increasingly new set of business and technology skills from tomorrow’s workforce. This is why SAP supports students in developing sought-after SAP skills, preparing them for job opportunities worldwide.

Get Involved

If you teach in areas related to AI, business informatics, information systems, or enterprise architecture,

  • Blog post from Yuri on Teaching Agentic AI with SAP
  • Blog post from Philip on applying SAP Signavio in an academic context
  • Blog post from Philip on SAP LeanIX
  • SAP Signavio for academic institutions landing page

 

Also join one of our SAP Academic Community Conference events around the globe throughout 2026. These are a good opportunity to experience agentic AI yourself and exchange with peer lecturers already integrating these topics their teaching.

entry.title: Improving RAG Retrieval with Cohere Rerank on SAP AI Launchpad
entry.content:
If you’ve worked with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems before, you’ve probably seen this happen.

You ask a question.

Your vector database retrieves a bunch of “relevant” chunks.

A few of them are useful. Some are loosely related. And occasionally, one of the top results makes you wonder if the embedding model was having a bad day.

This is one of the biggest challenges with RAG applications. Even if the LLM itself is powerful, the final response is still heavily dependent on the quality of the retrieved context. Poor retrieval leads to poor answers.

Most modern RAG pipelines rely on vector similarity search for retrieval, and this is fine because embeddings are fast, scalable, and generally good at capturing semantic meaning. But vector similarity alone does not always guarantee that the most relevant result ends up at the top.

This is where reranking comes in, because it’s not your LLM that is always the problem.

Instead of directly sending retrieved chunks to the LLM, a reranker takes the initially retrieved results and reorders them based on how relevant they are to the query. In a lot of cases, this can significantly improve the quality of the final response without changing your base LLM itself.

SAP AI Launchpad now supports the Cohere Rerank API, making it easier to integrate reranking into enterprise AI workflows running on SAP AI Core. This allows developers to improve retrieval quality in their RAG pipelines while continuing to use their existing retrieval and generation setup.

In this blog, we’ll take a look at:

  • what reranking is
  • why it matters in RAG applications
  • the trade-offs involved
  • how to deploy the Cohere Rerank model on SAP AI Launchpad
  • and how to use the deployed endpoint in a real application flow

Let’s get started.

 

What is Reranking?

In a typical RAG pipeline, a user query is converted into an embedding and matched against embeddings stored in a vector database. The database then returns the top matching chunks based on vector similarity.

This works well because embeddings are fast and scalable, making them ideal for searching across large datasets like SAP Help documentation.

But vector similarity alone does not always guarantee that the most useful result appears at the top.

This is where reranking helps.

Instead of directly sending retrieved chunks to the LLM, a reranker takes the initial search results and scores them again based on relevance to the query. The chunks are then reordered before being passed to the LLM.

The flow usually looks something like this:

The reranker does not replace vector search. Vector search is still responsible for quickly narrowing down the search space, while the reranker improves the quality of the final retrieved context.

 

Vector Search vs Reranking

To understand why reranking improves retrieval quality, it helps to look at how it differs from traditional vector search.

In a vector search pipeline, both the user query and the stored chunks are converted into embeddings independently. Similarity between them is then calculated using metrics like cosine similarity.

This works well for finding semantically related content quickly, which is why vector databases are widely used in RAG systems.

However, semantic similarity does not always mean the retrieved chunk directly answers the user’s question.

For example, in a RAG application built on SAP Help documentation, a query like:

“How do I configure OAuth for SAP AI Core?”

might retrieve chunks related to authentication, destinations, API access, and OAuth setup. All of these are related to the query, but not all of them are equally useful.

Reranking approaches this differently.

Instead of comparing embeddings independently, the reranker evaluates the query and each retrieved chunk together before assigning a relevance score. This allows it to better understand query intent and improve the ordering of the retrieved results.

The following diagram gives a simplified comparison between traditional vector similarity search and reranking:

In large documentation systems like SAP Help, improving retrieval quality can significantly improve:

  • answer accuracy
  • contextual relevance
  • response consistency
  • token efficiency by reducing unnecessary context

Another important advantage is that reranking improves retrieval quality without requiring changes to the base LLM itself. In many cases, improving retrieval can have a larger impact than switching to a larger or more expensive model.

Of course, reranking also comes with trade-offs like additional latency and inference cost, which we’ll look at next.

Trade-offs of Using Reranking

If reranking improves retrieval quality so much, the obvious question is:
why not use it for every document directly?

The answer is scale.

Rerankers are significantly more expensive and computationally heavier than vector similarity search. Running a reranker across thousands or millions of chunks for every query would introduce too much latency and cost for most applications.

This is why reranking is typically used as a second-stage retrieval step.

The vector database first narrows down the search space by retrieving the top candidate chunks quickly using embeddings. The reranker then evaluates only those retrieved results in more detail and improves their ordering before they are sent to the LLM.

Even with this smaller candidate set, reranking still introduces:

  • an additional model call
  • increased latency
  • additional inference cost

Because of this, reranking is usually most useful in scenarios where retrieval quality matters more than absolute response speed, such as large enterprise documentation systems or knowledge-heavy RAG applications.

It is also important to remember that reranking is not a replacement for good retrieval practices. Proper chunking, embeddings, and retrieval strategies still matter. Reranking works best as a precision improvement layer on top of an already solid retrieval pipeline.

 

Deploying the Cohere Rerank Model on SAP AI Launchpad

To use the Cohere Rerank API, the first step is to create a deployment on SAP AI Launchpad.

Navigate to the ML Operations section in SAP AI Launchpad and create a new configuration for the Cohere rerank model.

Steps to create configuration:

  1. Configuration details

Note: any valid configuration can be given

  1. Input Parameters

Note: Input artifacts are not required for this. We can directly proceed to review and create the configuration.

Once the configuration is created, create a deployment using that configuration.

Wait for the deployment to start running. The URL for the deployment can be found in the deployment details tab by selecting the deployment of your choice.

At this point, the reranker is ready to be integrated into a RAG pipeline.

The next step is to send a request to the deployment endpoint and use it to rerank retrieved chunks before passing them to the LLM.

 

Using the Deployment Endpoint in a RAG Pipeline

Once the deployment is active, the deployment URL can be used to send reranking requests from your application.

In a typical RAG pipeline, reranking is usually not performed on the entire document set directly. Doing that would be far too expensive and computationally heavy for most applications.

Instead, the vector database is first used to retrieve a larger candidate set using embeddings and cosine similarity.

For example, if a traditional keyword-based search might return 5 directly matching chunks, a vector search might retrieve the top 20–25 semantically related chunks instead. This improves recall and reduces the chances of missing useful context.

The reranker is then used as a second-stage retrieval step to improve precision.

Instead of sending all 20–25 retrieved chunks directly to the LLM, the application sends them to the reranker, which evaluates their relevance to the user query and returns only the most useful results.

A sample reranking request using Python could look something like this:

import requests

deployment_url = "<DEPLOYMENT_URL>/rerank"
auth_token = "<AUTH_TOKEN>" ## this is the Auth Token for BTP obtained using your ai core client credentials
resource_group = "<RESOURCE_GROUP_ID>"

query = "How do I configure OAuth for SAP AI Core?"

## Sample documents obtained through cosine similarity
documents = [
    "Authentication concepts in SAP BTP and identity management.",
    "Steps to configure OAuth for SAP AI Core using service keys and client credentials.",
    "API access and token lifecycle management in SAP AI Core.",
    "Destination configuration setup in SAP BTP.",
    "Identity provider configuration and trust setup."
]

payload = {
    "model": "cohere-reranker-35",
    "top_n": 3,
    "query": query,
    "documents": documents
}

headers = {
    "AI-Resource-Group": resource_group,
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {auth_token}"
}

response = requests.post(
    deployment_url,
    json=payload,
    headers=headers
)

response.raise_for_status()

reranked_results = response.json()

print(reranked_results)

The reranked results can then be used to construct the final prompt sent to the LLM.

This two-stage retrieval approach works particularly well for large documentation systems where multiple chunks may appear semantically similar while still serving very different purposes.

 

Conclusion

Reranking is one of those additions to a RAG pipeline that seems small at first, but can have a surprisingly large impact on retrieval quality.

In a lot of cases, the problem is not that the LLM is bad. The problem is that the right context never made it into the prompt in the first place.

By combining vector search with reranking, it becomes possible to balance both recall and precision in retrieval pipelines. The vector database retrieves broadly relevant chunks quickly, while the reranker helps identify which of those chunks are actually useful.

And when working with large documentation systems like SAP Help, that additional filtering layer can make a very noticeable difference.

With Cohere Rerank now available in SAP AI Launchpad, integrating reranking into enterprise RAG workflows becomes much more straightforward.

Sometimes, better answers do not come from a bigger model.
They come from better retrieval.

Happy coding! 🙂

entry.title: How Growing Businesses Simplify Complex Operations with an Integrated ERP System
entry.content:
In the world of commerce, business growth often brings increased operational complexity. What once worked well for a small team—spreadsheets, point solutions, and manual processes—can quickly become inefficient and difficult to manage as the company expands.

For many growing businesses, this shift is gradual. What starts as a flexible setup soon turns into daily firefighting—reconciling data, tracking inventory manually, and waiting on reports from multiple teams.

As organizations grow, many leaders begin looking for better ways to manage operations, improve visibility, and maintain efficiency. This is where an integrated Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system can make a significant difference.

By connecting key business functions into one platform, an ERP system helps growing businesses simplify operations and gain better control over their processes.

The Business Problem: Disconnected Systems and Data Silos

In their early growth stages, companies often use different tools to manage various business functions. One software might handle accounting, another inventory, while a CRM manages sales activities—and operational data may still be stored in spreadsheets. Although this approach seems manageable at first, it gradually creates operational challenges that impact clarity and efficiency:

  • Disconnected systems – Departments work on different platforms that do not communicate with each other
  • Data stored in silos – Important information is spread across multiple systems and files
  • Frequent data errors – Manual data entry increases the likelihood of mistakes and inconsistencies
  • Overdependence on spreadsheets – Teams rely heavily on Excel for reporting, tracking, and reconciliation

Over time, what once felt manageable becomes difficult to control, often leading to confusion, delays, and limited visibility for decision-making.

The Consequences of Fragmented Operations

When business systems are not integrated, everyday operations can quickly become time-consuming and error-prone. While the impact may not be obvious initially, inefficiencies grow as the business scales.

Some common challenges include:

  • 1. Data duplication
    The same data is entered into multiple systems, consuming valuable time and often leading to costly discrepancies.
  • 2. Inventory mismatches
    Without centralized visibility, stock records may not match actual inventory—leading to stockouts, overstocking, or delayed deliveries.
  • 3. Slow decision-making
    Generating reports requires manual consolidation, delaying access to accurate insights when they are needed most.
  • 4. Process inefficiency
    Manual workflows create bottlenecks in approvals, reporting, and day-to-day operations.

These issues not only affect internal productivity but also impact customer experience and overall business performance.

Why These Problems Occur as Businesses Grow

As businesses expand, operational complexity increases naturally. A growing company must manage:

  • Multiple departments
  • Large customer and vendor databases
  • Expanding product catalogues
  • More complex supply chains
  • Higher transaction volumes

Without integrated systems, managing these moving parts through disconnected tools becomes increasingly difficult. Teams end up spending more time reconciling information than using it to make strategic decisions.

Eventually, many businesses reach a stage where they need a unified system that provides a single, reliable source of truth across the organization.

Signs Your Business May Need an ERP System

Many growing businesses notice operational strain as they scale. If your team frequently encounters the following situations, it may indicate that your current systems are limiting your growth:

  • Heavy reliance on spreadsheets and manual reporting
  • Difficulty tracking inventory accurately
  • Multiple software systems that do not communicate with each other
  • Lack of real-time visibility into financial or operational data
  • Increasing time spent reconciling data across departments

When these challenges start affecting efficiency and decision-making, it is often a strong signal that an integrated ERP system may be needed.

Before ERP vs After ERP

The difference between operating with disconnected systems and an integrated ERP can be significant:

Without ERPWith Integrated ERP
Multiple disconnected systemsSingle unified platform
Manual data entryAutomated data flow
Delayed reportingReal-time insights
Inventory discrepanciesAccurate inventory visibility
Limited collaborationCross-departmental visibility

How an Integrated ERP System Solves These Challenges

An integrated ERP system connects key business functions into a single platform, enabling organizations to operate more efficiently and with greater clarity.

Instead of relying on multiple disconnected tools, businesses can access real-time operational and financial data from one centralized system.

Key benefits include:

1. Centralized data management

All business information—from finance and sales to inventory and procurement—is stored in one system, eliminating data silos and ensuring consistency.

2. Improved accuracy

Automated data flows reduce manual entry, minimizing errors and rework.

3. Real-time visibility

Decision-makers gain instant access to accurate reports and dashboards, enabling faster and more confident decisions.

4. Streamlined processes

Automated workflows reduce bottlenecks and improve overall efficiency.

5. Better collaboration

Teams across departments can work with shared, up-to-date information, improving coordination and execution.

For many growing organizations, ERP becomes the foundation for scaling operations while maintaining control and visibility.

How SAP Business One Addresses These Challenges Across Industries

While the need for integration is common across businesses, the nature of operational challenges varies by industry. Solutions like SAP Business One are designed specifically for small and mid-sized businesses, helping them streamline processes while remaining flexible and scalable.

Here’s how this translates across different sectors:

Manufacturing: Improving Production Planning and Visibility

As manufacturing businesses grow, managing production becomes more complex. Common challenges include:

  • Difficulty aligning production schedules with available inventory
  • Limited visibility into raw material requirements
  • Manual tracking of work orders
  • Poor coordination between procurement, production, and sales

An integrated ERP system brings structure to these processes. With SAP Business One, manufacturers can manage bill of materials (BOMs), material requirements planning (MRP), and production schedules within a single system—ensuring materials are available when needed and production stays aligned with demand.

Distribution and Trading: Achieving Real-Time Inventory Control

For distributors and trading companies, inventory management becomes increasingly challenging across multiple locations.

Common issues include:

  • Mismatches between system records and actual stock
  • Difficulty tracking stock movement
  • Limited visibility into fast- and slow-moving items
  • Inefficient order fulfilment

With SAP Business One, businesses gain real-time visibility into inventory across warehouses, enabling better demand planning and more reliable order commitments.

FMCG and e-Commerce: Managing High-Volume, Multi-Channel Operations Efficiently

FMCG and e-Commerce businesses often face increased complexity as they scale across multiple sales channels, warehouses, and customer touchpoints.

Challenges include:

  • Inconsistent inventory data across warehouses and online platforms
  • Difficulty consolidating sales, returns, and financial data across channels
  • Stock-outs or overstocking of fast-moving products
  • Limited visibility into demand patterns and customer buying behaviour

An integrated ERP system centralizes data across all channels, enabling businesses to manage inventory more effectively, streamline order fulfilment, manage order returns, and make faster, data-driven decisions around demand planning, pricing, and promotions.

Service-Oriented Businesses: Improving Project and Financial Management

Service organizations such as consulting or IT firms often struggle with:

  • Tracking project costs and profitability
  • Coordinating teams and resources
  • Delays in invoicing
  • Limited visibility into project progress

With SAP Business One, businesses can integrate project tracking, financials, and resource planning—providing a clear view of project performance and improving overall control.

What to Consider Before Choosing an ERP System

Selecting the right ERP system is a critical decision that can directly impact how effectively your business scales. Beyond features, it’s important to evaluate how well the solution aligns with your current needs and future growth plans.

Key considerations include:

  • Scalability Can the system support your business as it grows in terms of users, transactions, and operational complexity?
  • Industry relevance Does the ERP offer functionalities that align with your industry processes, such as manufacturing, distribution, retail, or services?
  • Ease of use and adoption Is the system intuitive enough for your teams to use effectively without extensive dependency on manual workarounds?
  • Real-time visibility Does it provide accurate, real-time insights across finance, inventory, and operations?
  • Integration capabilities Can it connect seamlessly with your existing tools and future systems?
  • Implementation and support Do you have access to the right expertise to ensure smooth implementation and ongoing optimization?

Solutions like SAP Business One ERP are designed with these considerations in mind. Built specifically for small and mid-sized businesses, it offers a balance of functionality, flexibility, and scalability—while integrating core business processes into a single, easy-to-use platform.

Bringing It All Together

As businesses grow, managing operations through disconnected systems becomes increasingly challenging. An integrated ERP system addresses this by centralizing data, improving visibility, and streamlining processes across departments.

The result is a more structured, efficient, and scalable way to manage operations—without the need for constant manual intervention.

Is Your Business Ready for ERP?

If your current systems are creating inefficiencies or limiting visibility, it may be time to evaluate whether your business is ready for ERP.

At RPM Infotech, we work with growing businesses to assess their existing processes, identify gaps, and recommend the right ERP approach based on their specific needs. We combine domain expertise with a practical understanding of business operations to ensure smooth implementation, faster adoption, and long-term success.

Start with an ERP evaluation for your business—and take the first step toward more streamlined and scalable operations.

entry.title: Re: SAP-Voyager: How OpenAI can take control of your SAP GUI
entry.content:
This is a really interesting example of how AI agents are starting to interact with enterprise software like SAP.

The idea behind SAP-Voyager is not that OpenAI “hacks” or magically controls SAP. Instead, it uses AI + automation frameworks to interact with the SAP GUI the same way a human user would — clicking buttons, entering data, navigating screens, and executing workflows through natural language commands.

What makes this exciting is that it combines:

Generative AI
AI agents
RPA-style automation
Vision AI
SAP GUI interaction

So instead of hardcoded scripts, users can potentially say things like:

“Create a purchase order”
or
“Post an invoice in SAP”

…and the AI agent attempts to execute the process automatically.

What I find most interesting is that this shows where enterprise automation is heading:

from rule-based automation
toward agentic AI systems that can reason, adapt, and recover from errors.

At the same time, this does not mean AI can fully replace SAP consultants or enterprise teams anytime soon. SAP environments are extremely complex, highly customized, and deeply tied to compliance, approvals, governance, and business rules. Even SAP professionals on Reddit point out that the real bottleneck is often messy enterprise data and process complexity — not the AI model itself.

Another important point is reliability. Traditional RPA tools can break easily when UI layouts change. AI-driven agents try to overcome that using reasoning and contextual understanding, but they still require strong governance, monitoring, and security controls.

Overall, projects like SAP-Voyager are a glimpse into the future of enterprise AI:

AI agents interacting with legacy enterprise systems
Natural-language-driven automation
Reduced manual SAP GUI work
Smarter workflow orchestration

But in real enterprise environments, success will still depend on:

clean data
governance
security
workflow design
and human oversight

The AI model is powerful, but enterprise execution is always the harder part.

entry.title: SmartRecruiters Migration Hub (2026 releases) – how are partners positioning it with customers and
entry.content:
Hi community,

I’m currently involved in several SF Recruiting → SmartRecruiters transition discussions, and I’m trying to better understand how partners are approaching the topic of the SmartRecruiters Migration Hub, which is being released progressively throughout 2026, starting mid‑May.

I’ve found some high‑level explanatory videos, but very limited partner‑ready written or slide material that can be reused directly with customers to explain:

  • what the Migration Hub will support in each release,
  • what it does not replace in terms of functional analysis and project work,
  • and how to position it without creating the expectation of a “push‑button” migration.

I’m particularly interested in hearing from partners on the following points:

  • How are you currently presenting the Migration Hub roadmap to customers who are asking whether they should migrate now vs wait for later releases?
  • Do you treat the Migration Hub primarily as a future accelerator, or are you already factoring it operationally into transition projects?
  • From a commercial and estimation standpoint:
    • does the existence of the Migration Hub materially change the way you estimate SF Recruiting → SmartRecruiters transition projects today?
    • are you seeing effort levels that are significantly different from a SmartRecruiters greenfield implementation, or is the baseline still broadly similar with migration‑specific assumptions and constraints?
  • Has anyone already built internal slides, customer‑safe messaging, or reusable content to explain this phased Migration Hub approach?

More generally, I’d be very interested in any early feedback or lessons learned on how to talk about this facilitator with customers, especially in pre‑sales contexts where a fixed number of days is expected, but tooling capabilities are still evolving.

Thanks in advance for sharing your experience, I really appreciate how helpful this community is !

entry.title: CEO Data Missing After Update Worksheet in SAP SuccessFactors Annual Bonus Template
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue in SAP SuccessFactors Compensation while working with the Annual Bonus process.

Scenario

  • CEO data is successfully imported through Employee History.
  • The record is available before running the Update Worksheet process.
  • After executing Update Worksheet, the CEO data is missing from the final Annual Bonus calculation report/template.

Observation

We noticed that the CEO does not have a second manager assigned in the hierarchy.

Because of this, we are unsure whether:

  • the worksheet update process is skipping the CEO record,
  • or if there is any hierarchy limitation/standard behavior in SuccessFactors Compensation.

Questions

  1. How should bonus calculation be handled for CEO records when there is no second manager in the hierarchy?
  2. What is the recommended approach to manage CEO records during the Update Worksheet process?
  3. Is there any standard SAP SuccessFactors configuration or workaround for this scenario?
  4. Has anyone faced a similar issue with executive/CEO data disappearing after Update Worksheet?

Any suggestions or guidance would be very helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: How to use BAPE_SDSALESDOCITEM to update custom VBAP fields?
entry.content:
Goal: Update the sales order from the reference document when reference document is updated.
Context: Sales Order is created using "Create with reference". Tried to debug this an found no error but I think I was not mapping it to proper line item. 

Additionally, the values of IT_CONTRACT_VBAP comes from other METHOD that select custom fields from VBAP.

This the how the BUILD_ITEM_EXTENSION is called:

BUILD_ITEM_EXTENSION(
EXPORTING
IV_SO_VBELN = IV_SO_VBELN
IV_CONTRACT_VBELN = IV_CONTRACT_VBELN
IV_CONTRACT_VBAP = IT_CONTRACT_VBAP
IMPORTING
ET_EXTENSION = LT_ITEM_EXT ).

DATA: lo_bapi_mapping TYPE REF TO if_cfd_bapi_mapping.

TYPES BEGIN OF ty_so_ref, vgbel TYPE vbap-vgbel, vgpos TYPE vbap-vgpos, posnr TYPE vbap-posnr, END OF ty_so_ref.

DATA lt_so_ref TYPE HASHED TABLE OF ty_so_ref WITH UNIQUE KEY vgbel vgpos.

CLEAR: et_extensionin.

SELECT vgbel, vgpos, posnr FROM VBAP WHERE VBELN = _so_vbeln AND uepos =  AND vgbel <>  AND vgpos <>  INTO TABLE (lt_so_vbap).

LOOP AT LT_SO_VBAP ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<LFS_SO_VBAP>).
 INSERT VALUE ty_so_ref( vgbel = <lfs_so_vbap>-vgbel vgpos = <lfs_so_vbap>-vgpos posnr = <lfs_so_vbap>-posnr ) INTO TABLE lt_so_ref. ENDLOOP.

lo_bapi_mapping = cl_fd_bapi_mapping=>get_instance( ).

LOOP AT it_contract_vbap ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<lfs_contract_item>).

DATA(lv_contract_vbeln) = <lfs_contract_item>-vbeln.
DATA(lv_contract_posnr) = CONV vbap-vgpos( |{ <lfs_contract_item>-posnr ALPHA = IN } | ).

TRY.
 DATA(ls_so_ref) = lt_so_ref[ vgbel = lv_contrat_vbeln vgpos = lv_contract_posnr ].
 CATCH cx_sy_itab_line_not_found.
  CONTINUE.
ENDTRY.
ENDLOOP.

DATA(ls_item_ext) = VALUE bape_sdsalesdocitem( 
 key = CONV sdsalesdoc_key( iv_so_vbeln )
).

ls_item_ext-data-z_field_A = <lfs_contract_item>-z_field_A.
ls_item_ext-data-z_field_B = <lfs_contract_item>-z_field_B.

ls_item_ext-datax-z_field_A_ = abap_true.
ls_item_ext-datax-z_field_B = abap_true.

TRY.
 lo_bapi_mapping->map_to_bapiparex_single(
 EXPORTING
  ir_source_structure = REF #( ls_item_ext )
 CHANGING
  ct_bapiparex = et_extensionin
 CATCH cx_cfd_bapi_mapping.
  CONTINUE.
 ENDTRY.
ENDLOOP.

entry.title: Five lessons from SAP's own transformation to an agentic AI enterprise
entry.content:
Becoming an AI first enterprise is a new challenge for all organizations and it is fundamentally different to problems we have solved before. It requires new creative solutions, fast testing and learning. In this session we share our top 5 lessons learned from driving AI adoption and excellence, what challenges we faced and what was our path to success. We will share details on how SAP’s Enterprise AI Operating Model keeps the agentic AI landscape lean and brings clarity and coherence in a rapidly evolving topic. You will gain insights into SAP's agentic AI Golden Path - a comprehensive framework for building, deploying, and governing enterprise-grade AI agents on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) at scale.

Speaker: Majeed Malik, Lead of Enterprise AI Product & Partner Management 

Registration: https://events.sap.com/sap-runs-sap/en_us/home.html

Live on YouTube: https://youtube.com/live/f5QdxEi7Hq4?feature=share

entry.title: Why Software Today Feels Like the Auto Industry Before Tesla
entry.content:
In my role as a Platform Product Manager at SAP, I see every day how the software industry is entering a phase that strongly resembles the moment when the automotive industry shifted from combustion engines to electric and software-defined vehicles. On the surface, we see incremental improvements. In reality, a fundamental platform shift is happening...

....and it is leading us towards something even bigger: The Autonomous Enterprise.

What the “skateboard” platform and software-defined vehicle were for the auto industry is, in many ways, what AI-first, platform-centric architectures are becoming for enterprise software. At SAP, this is shaping our business and platform strategy. From Business AI and Joule to the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) as the backbone of intelligent, end-to-end business processes and, ultimately, more autonomous business operations.

From Feature Software to AI-First Business Platforms

In the automotive industry, the real game changer was not just the battery. It was the completely rethought vehicle platform: centralized computing, over-the-air updates, and the idea that value creation truly starts after the car leaves the factory.

Enterprise software is undergoing a similar transition. We are moving from isolated feature development in individual applications to building platforms where data, processes and AI are tightly integrated. SAP BTP plays a central role as the data, integration and AI layer across core processes such as finance, supply chain and HR. AI is no longer “just another module”, but an integral part of how we design the platform: from embedded Business AI and Joule to AI agents that orchestrate end-to-end business scenarios and automate decisions.

Organizations that simply “bolt on” an AI feature to existing products are effectively doing what some OEMs did when they tried to electrify a combustion chassis: it works at first, but it does not scale and it does not fully exploit the potential of a new platform.

From Software-Defined Vehicles to the Autonomous Enterprise

If electrification and software-defined vehicles were the first big step, autonomous driving is the next logical stage in the automotive transformation. It builds on the same platform foundation - sensors, connectivity, centralized compute - but moves the value proposition from “assisted driver” to “self-driving system”.

The same pattern is now emerging in the enterprise:

  • AI first made individual processes “assisted”, through recommendations, anomaly detection, and copilots embedded in workflows.

  • The next step is autonomous processes that can sense, decide and act across systems: think of an order that triggers fulfillment, adjusts capacity, renegotiates freight and updates the forecast without human intervention. With humans supervising and steering, not micro-managing.

  • The long-term vision is an autonomous enterprise: a company that continuously optimizes itself based on real-time data, policies and goals, orchestrated by AI and agents running on a robust platform.

Just as autonomous driving requires a software-defined vehicle as its base, the autonomous enterprise requires a software-defined business: clean data, consistent process models and a platform that can host intelligent agents and automation at scale.

The Shift in Value Creation – From Hardware to Services, From Apps to Platforms, From Automation to Autonomy

In the automotive world, value creation is shifting from one-time hardware sales to software functions, data-driven services and mobility ecosystems.

We are seeing a similar pattern in enterprise software, with an additional twist:

  • From license-centric thinking to cloud subscriptions and platform consumption.

  • From isolated IT projects to AI-enabled, end-to-end business processes on a unified platform.

  • From task-level automation to goal-driven autonomy, where systems can propose and execute actions in line with business objectives and guardrails.

Here, SAP has a structural advantage: we are deeply embedded in mission-critical business processes around the globe and have access to highly relevant, trusted business data. Exactly the context that enterprise AI and autonomous scenarios need to be meaningful rather than experimental.

Platform Thinking from a Platform Product Manager’s Perspective

As a Platform Product Manager, I work right at this intersection. We no longer look at integration, APIs, events, data and AI services as a loose toolbox, but as a coherent business platform on which customers and partners can build their own innovation....and progressively more autonomous capabilities.

In practice, this means:

  • Designing and Evolving SAP BTP as an open, enterprise-grade foundation for AI and agentic use cases, not a closed island. Agents need a consistent way to access data, call business APIs and trigger workflows across systems.

  • Treating integration and data quality as the backbone : without a clean integration and data layer, AI remains a demo and autonomy remains a slideware vision.

  • Creating a developer and platform experience where APIs, events, AI services, rules and extensibility tell a single story: sensing events, reasoning over data and acting through workflows and business objects.

In other words: if software-defined vehicles needed a “central compute brain”, the autonomous enterprise needs a business platform brain – and that is where the platform strategy becomes truly differentiating.

To make this more tangible, consider the Order‑to‑Cash (O2C) process as the enterprise equivalent of the path from driver assistance to autonomous driving.

A concrete autonomous enterprise scenario - Order‑to‑Cash as the “autonomous driving” lane : In a first stage, AI improves individual steps: classifying incoming orders, predicting demand, suggesting risk scores, or flagging payment issues. This is similar to Level‑1/2 driver assistance in a car: helpful, but the driver (the user) is still in full control.

With agentic AI on/with SAP BTP and Joule, the pattern shifts: AI agents can monitor the entire O2C flow end‑to‑end, reason over real‑time business data and take actions across systems. For example:

  • An Order Reliability Agent continuously scans open sales orders, checks supply, lead times, logistics constraints and predicts which orders are at risk.

  • If a risk is detected, the agent automatically proposes mitigation options like alternative plants, substitute materials, different carriers or adjusted delivery dates, based on company policies and service levels.

  • A Cash Management or Disputes Agent can, in parallel, reconcile incoming payments, detect anomalies and automatically trigger follow‑ups or dispute workflows, reducing manual reconciliation effort dramatically.news.

From a platform perspective, SAP BTP provides the build, integration, data, workflow and AI services that these agents rely on: APIs to access S/4HANA or CX systems, eventing for business changes, AI engines for prediction and workflow/rules for execution. Joule then acts as the orchestrator and interface: business users can ask natural‑language questions (“Which high‑value orders are at risk this week?”) and approve, override or refine the actions that agents proposes.

This is very close to how autonomous driving stacks are built:

  • Sensors and maps in a car correspond to operational and planning data across ERP, CX and supply chain.

  • The driving policy engine corresponds to business rules, guardrails and objectives (service levels, margin, risk).

  • The autonomous driving controller corresponds to AI agents on SAP BTP and Joule, which continuously sense, decide and act across multiple “lanes” of the enterprise.

The endpoint is not a “hands‑off” company, but an autonomous enterprise where a large share of O2C, source‑to‑pay and plan‑to‑produce runs system‑driven. And people focus on exceptions, design and strategy. Just like a driver in an autonomous vehicle, humans stay in charge of the destination and constraints, while the platform handles the repetitive steering in between.

Legacy Thinking vs. AI- and Autonomy-First Architecture

The key leadership question, in my view, is not: “Where can we quickly add some AI?”

Instead, it is: “What would our product and platform architecture look like if we rebuilt it tomorrow, AI- and autonomy-first, from a clean slate...using the business processes and data we already have?”

A few principles I consider critical, for us and for our customers:

  • Architecture before features: Rethink the platform model first. Data, events, policies and agents...then add capabilities on top.

  • Data and model strategy at the core: Without a clear data foundation, governance and feedback loops, AI and autonomy will stay isolated pilot projects.

  • Platform and ecosystem mindset: Openness, APIs and co-innovation with partners and developers are the lever for scale. No single vendor will “own” all agents or all models.

  • AI embedded, not bolted on: Design AI and agents as a natural layer across processes, data and user journeys, with humans in control and clear guardrails.

  • From workflows to policies and goals: Move from hard-coded workflows towards systems that can optimize against business goals within defined policies. Similar to how autonomous driving optimizes route, speed and safety within traffic rules.

A Question for Platform Leaders on the Road to the Autonomous Enterprise

The automotive industry has learned the hard way what happens when a platform shift is underestimated. In enterprise software, we have the opportunity to avoid repeating that mistake and to use the current AI wave as a stepping stone towards the autonomous enterprise.

If you are responsible for platforms or products...at SAP, on the customer side or in the partner ecosystem, ask yourself two simple questions:

  1. Which parts of your platform would you not build the same way again if you had to start tomorrow, AI-first, from scratch?

  2. What would you need to change so that your systems can not only automate tasks, but autonomously optimize outcomes under clear business guardrails?

That is where your real transformation roadmap begins and the journey we will continue at SAP Sapphire this year, where the next chapter of SAP BTP’s evolution, from intelligent platform to true autonomous enterprise backbone, will come into focus.

Curious to hear:
What would you change in your platform today if your goal was not just automation, but a truly autonomous enterprise tomorrow?

SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule Joule Studio #AutonomousEnterprise SAP Sapphire SAP Business AI

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: Cannot Transport Existing Custo...
entry.content:
Hello @alex_dsouza 


If you only changed field length, it is treated as a non-transport-relevant change in S/4HANA Cloud → it will NOT appear in Export Software Collection.


Ensure you “Publish” and “Enable Usage” for the custom field
Add the Business Context (the parent object) to the software collection (not the field itself)
If still not visible → create a new version/change affecting metadata (e.g., label or usage), then publish again


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: How to access custom VBAK fields in BAPI_SALESORDER_C...
entry.content:
Hello @bigflatfoot 


Not possible via BAPE_VBAK in S/4HANA Cloud

Use EXTENSIONIN (only if field is API-enabled)
Or use OData API (API_SALES_ORDER_SRV)

Custom Fields app fields are not added to BAPI structures.


Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: ABAP Objects: How to Properly Reset an Instance to It...
entry.content:
Hello @czvn97 


In ABAP Objects, there is no standard free( ) method for every object.
Use this to remove the object reference
CLEAR lo_object.

lo_object->free( ) works only if your class actually defines a method named FREE.

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Create multiple task lists with service packages, wit...
entry.content:
Hello @ABS21 


Use this skeleton inside loop. Important: call EAM_TASKLIST_POST + BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT for each task list to avoid buffer/service package mixing. EAM_TASKLIST_CREATE creates data in buffer; posting/commit writes it to DB

DATA: lt_return TYPE bapiret2_t.

DO 2 TIMES.

CLEAR: ls_header,
lt_operations,
lt_spacks_outlines,
lt_spacks_lines,
lt_return.

"Header
ls_header-plnty = 'A'. "General task list
ls_header-werks = '1000'.
ls_header-ktext = |Task list { sy-index }|.

"Operation
APPEND VALUE #( vornr = '0010'
steus = 'PM03'
arbpl = 'MECH'
werks = '1000'
ltxa1 = 'Service operation' ) TO lt_operations.

"Service package outline
APPEND VALUE #( vornr = '0010'
packno = sy-index ) TO lt_spacks_outlines.

"Service line
APPEND VALUE #( packno = sy-index
extrow = '0000000010'
srvpos = 'SERVICE_CODE'
menge = '1'
meins = 'AU' ) TO lt_spacks_lines.

CALL FUNCTION 'EAM_TASKLIST_CREATE'
EXPORTING
is_header = ls_header
iv_date = sy-datum
IMPORTING
ev_plnnr = lv_plnnr
ev_plnal = lv_plnal
TABLES
it_operations = lt_operations
it_spacks_outlines = lt_spacks_outlines
it_spacks_lines = lt_spacks_lines
et_return = lt_return.

READ TABLE lt_return WITH KEY type = 'E' TRANSPORTING NO FIELDS.
IF sy-subrc = 0.
CONTINUE.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'EAM_TASKLIST_POST'
TABLES
et_return = lt_return.

CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
EXPORTING
wait = abap_true.

ENDDO.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: SAP S4Hana Public Cloud - Custom Analytical Queries a...
entry.content:
Thank you for bringing up this question.
In the new application Custom Analytical Queries & Services, date functions are treated like any other derivation functions, without special handling. To proceed, create a parameter of type Date. Select the parameter -> "Filter with derivation functions" and choose the appropriate lookup entity—either F_GREGORIANCALDTEFUNCRNGEVAL or F_GREGORIANCALDTEFUNCSGLVAL would be fitting options. You can pick "P_DateFunction" from the value help. 

Apply this parameter in the filters tab for the dimension.
